Fax: 312–922–6798

Arnold Goldberg  

July 28, 1999. Charles Brenner honors me by taking issue with a point I made in my article about the meaninglessness of asserting the universality of compromise formation. However, I hope my response to him can be seen as part of a much broader issue in science, one that I have addressed in The Prisonhouse of Psychoanalysis (1990) and that perhaps finds its best formulation in Karl Popper (1994).

Brenner's position seems to be that all mental acts are compromise formations constructed from our psychic agencies. It therefore seems fair to say that his letter is a compromise formation, just as are my response and, indeed, my commentary on this response. One cannot ever swing free of this network of theoretical explanation, but after a while it ceases to be a matter of concern or interest.

876

responseSet.title: How Central is the Analysis of Aggression to Clinical Psychoanalysis?
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How Central is the Analysis of Aggression to Clinical Psychoanalysis?

Michael Singer

Michael Singer

15 Bond Street
Great Neck, NY 11021
Fax: 516-829-6858
E-mail: Mhsinger@mindspring.com

Michael Singer  

Erik Gann reminded us that in today's climate, when the basic framework of psychoanalytic theory, especially the theory of technique, is undergoing major challenges and revisions, the “disorienting” title of this panel is quite relevant. And since our new tendency increasingly is to orient ourselves around interrelational phenomena, such questions as those implied in the panel's title may even lead us “to make different assumptions about what makes people tick,” and how to help them analytically. This is especially so when we contemplate the intrapsychic and interpersonal roles of aggression—particularly with regard to where and whom it comes from in the analytic dyad.

Turning the title upside down, in effect, Gann asked the panelists to consider whether, and how much, recent developments in the theory of clinical process and technique have influenced their work with the psychoanalysis of aggression. He also asked how each of them “conceptualizes the sources and manifestations of aggression, destructiveness, sadism, hostility, hate, rage, and so on.”

Gann thinks that we probably find it difficult to work with our patients' aggression and raw destructive urges, and that we may even discourage them. He wondered if the current emphasis in the psychoanalytic theory of technique on “shared responsibility, relatedness,…development and alliance” enhance “our successful encounter with the basic, destructive aspects of our patients' pathologies? Or are we in one way or another damping down perhaps the darkest part of our enterprise?

Panel held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 21, 1997. Panelists: Erik Gann (chair), Raymond Friedman, Arnold Rothstein, Robert Caper, James Herzog.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Nevermore: The Hymen and the Loss of Virginity

Deanna Holtzman and Nancy Kulish. Northvale, NJ: Aronson, xiv + 249 pp., $37.50.

Ruth Lax

Ruth Lax

1185 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-348-2937
E-mail: Rlaxphd@aol.com

Ruth Lax  

Virginity, its significance, and the significance of its loss have received relatively scant discussion in psychoanalytic literature. This fact is surprising. It is important to note and examine it, given the value attached to virginity by most societies and considering the psychic consequences that follow the internalization of stereotypic attitudes by both females and males.

Holtzman and Kulish consider defloration and the state of not being a virgin in detail, from a historical and contemporary perspective as well as from a general psychoanalytic point of view, and especially its role in the specific subjective experience of the individual. The authors' findings indicate that for girls and women the loss of virginity constitutes an epic experience. It is a unique and “once in a lifetime” change that marks an ending as well as a passage to a new phase of womanhood and adult sexuality. The ending of virginity has emotional reverberations, specific for each individual. It bears a profound psychic significance that may be acknowledged, denied, or even repressed. Nonetheless, as Holtzman and Kulish so explicitly note, the fact remains: once virginity is lost, it is Nevermore.

Many questions can be raised about the meanings of virginity. The authors with great skill pursue their investigation of this topic into areas of etymology, folklore, myth, fairy tale, anthropology, biography, literature, metaphor, and symbolism. The concept of “never” echoes in each of these areas, confirming Holtzman and Kulish's hypothesis of the finality associated with defloration. It is “like birth and death, a one-time occurrence that cannot be repeated” (p. 3).

The authors raise the questions: what constitutes virginity? Is it a purely anatomic condition? Does it imply no sexual experience? How is masturbation related to ideas about the hymen? Does fantasy with sexual themes impinge on feelings about virginity?

1461
